title: Multidisciplinary Conference - Anticoagulation Management Post-GI Hemorrhage
author: Dr. William Thompson

attendees.name: Dr. William Thompson
attendees.role: Gastroenterology — performed EGD, managing GI bleed

attendees.name: Dr. James Wilson
attendees.role: Cardiology — managing atrial fibrillation and heart failure

attendees.name: Dr. Sarah Chen
attendees.role: Primary Care Physician — longitudinal care, medication management

attendees.name: Dr. Emily Brown
attendees.role: Hospital Medicine — inpatient primary team (present for portion)

attendees.name: Dr. Rachel Kim, PharmD
attendees.role: Clinical Pharmacist — medication reconciliation and interaction review

sections.patientSummary:
Robert Morrison is a 72-year-old male with permanent atrial fibrillation (on warfarin since 2017), HFrEF (EF 30-35%), CKD stage 3b, type 2 diabetes, CAD (3-vessel disease, medically managed), and hypertension who was admitted on 9/12/2023 with life-threatening upper GI hemorrhage presenting as hemorrhagic shock (BP 88/52, Hgb 7.2, INR 3.8).

The hemorrhage was caused by a 1.5 cm gastric ulcer on the lesser curvature (Forrest IIa — visible vessel) in the setting of:
1. Concurrent NSAID use (ibuprofen 400 mg TID x 3 days, OTC, patient was unaware of risk)
2. Supratherapeutic warfarin anticoagulation (INR 3.8)
3. Newly diagnosed Helicobacter pylori infection

The patient required 4 units of packed RBCs, 2 days in the MICU, and endoscopic hemostasis with combination therapy (epinephrine + cautery + hemoclipping). He has recovered well and is clinically stable on the medicine floor. The purpose of this conference is to determine the long-term anticoagulation strategy.
sections.strokeRiskAssessment:
Dr. Wilson presented the stroke risk assessment:

CHA₂DS₂-VASc Score: 5 points
- Age 65-74: 1 point
- Hypertension: 1 point
- Diabetes mellitus: 1 point
- Heart failure (EF <40%): 1 point
- Vascular disease (CAD): 1 point
- Total: 5 points

This corresponds to an estimated annual stroke risk of approximately 6.7% without anticoagulation. Over a 5-year horizon, this represents a roughly 30% cumulative risk of thromboembolic stroke, many of which would be disabling or fatal in a patient of this age and comorbidity burden.

Dr. Wilson emphasized that Mr. Morrison's atrial fibrillation is permanent (not paroxysmal), his left atrium is dilated (4.8 cm), and he has significant left ventricular dysfunction — all of which independently increase thromboembolic risk beyond what the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score captures.
sections.bleedingRiskAssessment:
Dr. Thompson presented the bleeding risk assessment:

HAS-BLED Score: 5 points
- Hypertension (uncontrolled): 1 point
- Abnormal renal function (CKD 3b): 1 point
- Age >65: 1 point
- Drug use predisposing to bleeding (prior aspirin): 1 point
- Prior major bleeding (this admission): 1 point
- Total: 5 points

A HAS-BLED score ≥3 is considered high risk for major bleeding.

Additional bleeding risk factors not captured by the score:
- Newly diagnosed large gastric ulcer (1.5 cm) — requires 6-8 weeks minimum for healing
- Positive H. pylori (independent ulcer risk factor, eradication in progress)
- History of NSAID use suggesting patient may inadvertently re-expose himself to ulcerogenic agents
- CKD (uremic platelet dysfunction)
- Heart failure (hepatic congestion may impair warfarin metabolism)
- Concurrent need for at least low-dose aspirin for CAD

Rebleeding risk for Forrest IIa ulcers after successful endoscopic therapy is 5-10% within 30 days even with optimal medical therapy. This risk is significantly amplified if anticoagulation is resumed.
sections.discussionOfOptions:
The following anticoagulation strategies were discussed in detail:

OPTION 1: Resume warfarin after ulcer healing (6-8 weeks)
- Pros: Best stroke prevention for CHA₂DS₂-VASc 5. Well-established efficacy.
- Cons: Highest rebleed risk. Patient already demonstrated difficulty maintaining therapeutic INR (was 3.8). The index bleed was life-threatening (hemorrhagic shock, 4-unit transfusion, MICU admission). Adding warfarin back to a patient with a recent Forrest IIa ulcer, even after healing, carries substantial risk. Prior adherence concerns with concurrent NSAID use.
- Group consensus: Too high risk. DECLINED.

OPTION 2: Switch to a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) after ulcer healing
- Pros: DOACs (apixaban, edoxaban) have lower rates of GI bleeding compared to warfarin in atrial fibrillation trials (RE-LY, ARISTOTLE). No INR monitoring needed, fewer drug-food interactions, more predictable pharmacokinetics.
- Cons: Still carries meaningful GI bleed risk, particularly rivaroxaban and dabigatran. Apixaban has the most favorable GI bleeding profile among DOACs. However, even apixaban was felt to carry unacceptable risk given the severity of this hemorrhage. Additionally, patient has CKD 3b (eGFR ~35) — dose adjustments required, and renal function may continue to decline. Reversal agents (andexanet alfa for apixaban) are expensive and not universally available.
- Dr. Thompson's position: Even the lowest-risk DOAC (apixaban) carries a non-trivial rebleed risk in this patient. The index bleed was nearly fatal.
- Dr. Wilson's position: Acknowledged the bleeding risk but noted the stroke risk is substantial. Felt this was a reasonable option if bleeding risk modifiable factors were optimized.
- Group consensus: Considered but ultimately DECLINED given severity of index bleed. Would reconsider only if patient has a stroke or TIA.

OPTION 3: Left atrial appendage (LAA) occlusion device (e.g., WATCHMAN)
- Pros: Provides stroke risk reduction comparable to warfarin long-term without chronic anticoagulation. Eliminates medication adherence issues.
- Cons: Requires a procedure (transesophageal echocardiography + catheterization). Short-term anticoagulation/dual antiplatelet therapy required post-implant (typically 45 days of DOAC or warfarin, then 6 months of dual antiplatelet). Patient has significant comorbidities that increase procedural risk. Patient's EF of 30-35% increases periprocedural risk.
- Patient preference: Discussed with Mr. Morrison and his wife. Patient expressed that he is "done with procedures" and does not wish to pursue an elective cardiac intervention at this time. He stated, "I've been through enough. I don't want another procedure unless it's absolutely necessary."
- Group consensus: Reasonable option but DECLINED by patient. Will revisit if clinical circumstances change (e.g., stroke, TIA, or if patient changes his mind).

OPTION 4: Discontinue all anticoagulation, start aspirin 81 mg for modest cardioprotection
- Pros: Eliminates the highest-risk medication for rebleeding. Aspirin 81 mg provides very modest stroke risk reduction in atrial fibrillation (~20% relative risk reduction vs ~65% with warfarin). Also provides secondary prevention for CAD. PPI co-therapy mitigates some of aspirin's GI risk.
- Cons: Substantially inferior stroke prevention compared to anticoagulation. Accepts a 5-6% annual stroke risk (vs ~2% on anticoagulation). Aspirin itself is not without GI bleeding risk, though far less than anticoagulation.
- Group consensus: This was felt to be the SAFEST option given the clinical circumstances.
sections.finalDecision:
CONSENSUS DECISION: PERMANENTLY DISCONTINUE WARFARIN. Start aspirin 81 mg daily for modest stroke risk reduction and CAD secondary prevention, with concomitant indefinite PPI therapy (pantoprazole 40 mg daily) for gastroprotection.

Rationale:
1. The index GI hemorrhage was life-threatening (hemorrhagic shock, Class III, 4-unit transfusion, MICU admission).
2. The patient has multiple ongoing, non-modifiable risk factors for GI bleeding: CKD, heart failure, age >70, prior major GI bleed, H. pylori (being treated).
3. While the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 5 indicates significant stroke risk, the team felt that the immediate risk of rebleeding (potentially fatal given his cardiac reserve) outweighed the annual stroke risk.
4. The patient declined the LAA occluder, which was the only option that could provide meaningful stroke protection without anticoagulation.
5. Aspirin 81 mg with PPI co-therapy represents the best compromise between some cardioprotection and acceptable bleeding risk.

This decision was made UNANIMOUSLY by all three physicians present.

IMPORTANT: This decision should be REVISITED if:
- Patient experiences a TIA or stroke (would strongly reconsider DOAC or LAA occluder)
- Patient changes his mind about the WATCHMAN device
- New evidence emerges that changes the risk-benefit calculus
- Repeat EGD shows complete ulcer healing and patient remains bleed-free for >12 months
sections.patientCounseling:
Mr. Morrison and his wife Patricia were counseled together at bedside immediately following the conference (3:30 PM).

Topics discussed:
1. The decision to permanently stop warfarin and the reasoning behind it.
2. The increased stroke risk without anticoagulation — explained in lay terms that there is approximately a 1 in 15 chance per year of having a stroke without blood thinners, compared to about 1 in 50 with blood thinners.
3. Stroke warning signs — the mnemonic BE-FAST was reviewed:
   - Balance: sudden loss of balance
   - Eyes: sudden vision changes
   - Face: facial drooping
   - Arms: arm weakness or numbness
   - Speech: slurred speech or difficulty speaking
   - Time: call 911 immediately
4. The importance of calling 911 immediately if stroke symptoms occur — emphasized that time to treatment is critical and that early treatment with clot-busting medication can prevent permanent disability.
5. The role of aspirin 81 mg — provides modest protection but is not a substitute for full anticoagulation.
6. The importance of lifelong PPI therapy to protect the stomach.
7. STRICT avoidance of all NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, meloxicam, etc.) — reinforced repeatedly. Use acetaminophen for pain.
8. The option of the WATCHMAN device remains available if he changes his mind.

Both Mr. Morrison and Mrs. Morrison verbalized understanding of all points. Mrs. Morrison took written notes. Patient stated he agrees with the decision and "would rather take my chances with a stroke than go through that bleeding again." He was reassured that this is a reasonable and medically sound decision.

Emotional status: Patient appeared accepting and relieved. Wife appeared anxious but understanding. Social work follow-up recommended.
followUpPlan: GI (Dr. Thompson): Repeat EGD in 4-6 weeks to confirm ulcer healing and confirm H. pylori eradication. Indefinite PPI. Annual GI assessment.
Cardiology (Dr. Wilson): Follow-up in 2 weeks for AF rate control assessment off anticoagulation. Will consider Holter monitor. Revisit LAA occluder discussion at next visit.
PCP (Dr. Chen): Follow-up in 1 week for medication reconciliation, renal function check, wound care. Long-term monitoring of stroke symptoms. Reinforce NSAID avoidance at every visit.
Pharmacy (Dr. Kim): Complete medication reconciliation before discharge. Ensure H. pylori regimen interactions managed. Patient education on all medication changes.

attestation:
This multidisciplinary conference note documents the discussion and consensus decision regarding Mr. Morrison's anticoagulation management. All attendees listed above have reviewed and co-signed this note to confirm the accuracy of the documentation and their agreement with the decision.

Primary author and electronically signed by: William Thompson, MD, FACG
Date/Time: 9/15/2023 4:00 PM

Co-signed by: James Wilson, MD, FACC — 9/15/2023 4:30 PM
Co-signed by: Sarah Chen, MD — 9/15/2023 5:00 PM
